coredata.dc:title: Antifungal molecular details of MNQ-derived novel carbon dots against Penicillium digitatum
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yongchun
Li, Wei
Wu, Keyue
Lei, Bingfu
Chen, Jianying
Zhang, Xiaoyong
Lei, Hongtao
Duan, Xuewu
Huang, Riming
coredata.dc:description: It is urgent to develop high-efficiency and low-toxicity natural antifungal agents on green mold caused by Penicillium digitatum. The effect of 2-methoxy-1, 4-naphthoquinone (MNQ) inhibition of P. digitatum was not very satisfactory. MNQ-derived carbon dots (MNQ-CDs) synthesized through a solvothermal route were used as antifungal agents against P. digitatum. The antifungal activity of prepared MNQ-CDswas enhanced compared to MNQ, and the minimum inhibitory concentration was 2.8 μg/mL. A total of 441 genes and 122 metabolites have undergone significant changes. The omics data revealed that MNQ-CDs primarily modified the metabolism of aromatic amino acids and synthesis of the cell membrane in P. digitatum, thereby inhibiting its propagation. Furthermore, compared with MNQ, MNQ-CDs had a better control effect on the green mold of citrus fruits, and could more significantly inhibit the propagation of P. digitatum. This study provides a new idea for the design of new and efficient antifungal materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: 2-methoxy-1
4-naphthoquinone
Antifungal mechanism
Carbon dots
Citrus
Omics analysis
Penicillium digitatum

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Article(s) from the Special Issue on Postharvesting chemistry and biochemistry of fruits and vegetables by Dr. Haiyan Gao and Dr. Senem Kamiloglu

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of MNQ-CDs
Effect of MNQ-CDs on green mold of citrus fruits caused by P. digitatum
Effect of MNQ-CDs on the control of green mold of citrus fruits caused by P. digitatum
Unexpected changes in the antifungal effect of MNQ-CDs
Antifungal mechanism of MNQ-CDs against P. digitatum

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China , grant number 32161160304 ; the Key-Area Research and Development Program of Guangdong Province, grant number 2020B1111030004 ; the Program of Department of Natural Resources of Guangdong Province, grant number GDNRC[ 2021]53, and the Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Province , grant number 2021GXNSFDA075010 .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Effect of MNQ-CDs at different concentrations on mycelial growth of P. digitatum incubated at 28 °C for 2 days. (B) SEM image of mycelia after 4 days of incubation with or without MNQ-CDs. D-C: mycelia treated without MNQ-CDs for 4 days; D-T: mycelia treated without MNQ-CDs for 2 days and with 2.8 µg/mL MNQ-CDs for another 2 days. In the red box of D-T, the mycelium surface collapsed severely and showed multiple folds. The structure was irregular and distorted, with convex and dry, and the sporangium atrophied. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

GO functional analysis and KEGG pathway analysis revealed some differential genes. (A) GO functional analysis in D-T (adjusted P < 0.05). The three major enriched GO categories were biological process, cellular component, and molecular function. (B) differential genes enriched in KEGG pathway analysis of differential genes in D-T (adjusted P < 0.05).
Shifts in metabolomics profiles. (A) Ratio of each class of Metabolites in D-T. (B) Enriched-KEGG pathways of differential metabolites in D-T.

Co-expression network analysis of differential genes and differential metabolites based on Pearson correlation. (A). Interaction network of DEG and DEM involved in alpha-Linolenic acid metabolism. (B). Interaction network of DEG and differential metabolites involved in riboflavin metabolism. (C). The interaction network of differential genes and differential metabolites related to aromatic amino acids metabolism. The edges colored in “red” and “green” represent positive and negative correlations, determined by Pearson's correlation coefficient = 1 or = -1, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Effect of the MNQ-CDs on decay diameter and decay rate of citrus fruit caused by P. digitatum. (A). Pictures of fruits 6 days after inoculation with P. digitatum. (B). Decay rate of different treatment groups for the same storage time. (C). Decay diameter of different treatment groups for the same storage time. Different lowercase letters indicate significant differences at the 0.05 level (p < 0.05).

TEM image of mycelia after 4 days of incubation with or without MNQ-CDs. D-C: mycelia treated without MNQ-CDs for 4 days; D-T: mycelia treated without MNQ-CDs for 2 days and with 2.8 µg/mL MNQ-CDs for another 2 days.

head.ce:title: Antifungal molecular details of MNQ-derived novel carbon dots against Penicillium digitatum

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors equally contributed to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is urgent to develop high-efficiency and low-toxicity natural antifungal agents on green mold caused by Penicillium digitatum. The effect of 2-methoxy-1, 4-naphthoquinone (MNQ) inhibition of P. digitatum was not very satisfactory. MNQ-derived carbon dots (MNQ-CDs) synthesized through a solvothermal route were used as antifungal agents against P. digitatum. The antifungal activity of prepared MNQ-CDswas enhanced compared to MNQ, and the minimum inhibitory concentration was 2.8 μg/mL. A total of 441 genes and 122 metabolites have undergone significant changes. The omics data revealed that MNQ-CDs primarily modified the metabolism of aromatic amino acids and synthesis of the cell membrane in P. digitatum, thereby inhibiting its propagation. Furthermore, compared with MNQ, MNQ-CDs had a better control effect on the green mold of citrus fruits, and could more significantly inhibit the propagation of P. digitatum. This study provides a new idea for the design of new and efficient antifungal materials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Citrus fruits are mainly produced in remote mountainous areas far from consumer markets, so they need to be stored for a certain time before being sold (Ouyang et al., 2016). During storage, citrus fruits are easily infected with fungal diseases such as penicillium, green mold, and acid rot. Among them, green mold diseases caused by P. digitatum can lead to postharvest losses of up to 90% of citrus in arid regions and subtropical climate zones (Cheng et al., 2020). At present, chemical fungicides such as Imazole, thiabendazole and pyrimethanil are used to control green mold diseases (Papoutsis et al., 2019). However, these synthetic chemical fungicides pose problems such as chemical residues, pathogen resistance risks, environmental pollution, and health hazards (Talibi et al., 2014). Natural compounds (e.g. thymus vulgaris, cinnamaldehyde, Peppermint oil and carvacrol) have been continuously applied in the biological control of postharvest diseases of fruits and vegetables in recent years (Mutlu-Ingok et al., 2021; Mamadalieva et al., 2020) due to their advantages of high efficiency, safety, non-toxicity and difficulty in inducing resistance to pathogenic bacteria (Wan et al., 2019) as compared to commercial fungicides and have great potential for application.

2-methoxy-1, 4-naphthoquinone (MNQ) can be extracted from Swertia calycina and Impatiens balsamina (Jiang et al., 2017), which has anticancer, antibacterial, antifungal, and antiallergic effects (Liew et al., 2015). Our previous studies have shown that MNQ has effective antifungal activity against many Penicillium species from citrus (Guo et al., 2020). Although MNQ inhibited Penicillium species from citrus, its inhibitory activity was not satisfactory and its mechanism of action was similar to that of essential oils, such as menthol, citronellol, and thymol (Swamy et al., 2016), which bacteriostatic effects may be the result of the synergistic effect of each component. MNQ should be modified in other ways to obtain compounds with better activity and novel mechanisms of action.

In recent years, carbon dots (CDs, novel fluorescent carbon nanomaterials with particle size<10 nm) have attracted extensive attention due to their small size, ease of preparation, functionalization, high water dispersibility and good biocompatibility (Lin et al., 2022). Li et al developed CDs using vitamin C as a carbon source (Li et al., 2018). These CDs derived from vitamin C are characterized by low toxicity and biodegradability, ultimately killing bacteria and fungi by damaging bacterial walls, disrupting secondary structures of DNA/ RNA of bacteria and fungi, and inhibiting the expression of important genes. They showed strong broad-spectrum antibacterial activity and antifungal activity. CDs synthesized by the Aspergillus foetidus showed very significant and species-specific antifungal activity results against Aspergillus pathogens (Nasrin and Das, 2016). Parya Ezati et al. synthesized CDs by a one-pot hydrothermal method using glucose as a carbon source and these CDs were used to prepare chitosan/gelatin-based composite films, which exhibited high antifungal activity against Aspergillus flavus and Colletotrichum orbiculare (Parya et al., 2022).

CDs have been synthesized in different ways over the past few years using different starting materials, such as microwave-mediated synthesis, water/solvothermal synthesis, template method, acid oxidation method, oxidation of candle soot, and pyrolysis of organic compounds (Jaleel & Pramod, 2018; Long et al., 2020). Hydrothermal synthesis is the most commonly used method to prepare carbon dots, because it is simple and efficient, and most of the CDs synthesized have good water solubility (Zhang et al., 2010).

In this experiment, to elucidate the effects and mechanisms of MNQ-derived carbon dots (MNQ-CDs) synthesized through a solvothermal route against P. digitatum, high-dimensional biological data transcriptomic and untargeted metabolomics were integrated to understand the differential genes and differential metabolites in P. digitatum after MNQ-CDs treated. At the same time, the control effect of MNQ-CDs on citrus green mold was verified, providing some theory and basis for controlling citrus postharvest green mold and inhibiting citrus postharvest pathogens.

MNQ used for this work was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (United States). Referring to the method of Chen et al. (2022), MNQ-CDs were synthesized by heat treatment with MNQ as carbon source and DMSO as solvent. At the same time, the reaction temperature (120 °C, 180 °C) is controlled below the decomposition temperature of the carbon source, to maintain its antifungal structure. For MNQ-CDs, MNQ (20 mg) and 10 mL of DMSO were ultrasound for 10 min and then introduced in 15 mL Teflon Reactor. And the reactor was placed in an oven and heated at 120 °C for 6 h. After that, the reactor was cooled at room temperature and clear yellow dispersion of MNQ-CDs was obtained. The obtained solution was freeze-dried to obtain dried MNQ-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of MNQ-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: P. digitatum used for this work was kindly provided by Professor MY Hu (College of Agriculture, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou, China) and it has been used in many studies (Gong et al., 2016; Xu et al., 2022). P. digitatum was preserved on potato dextrose agar at 28 °C. The effect of MNQ-CDs on the mycelial growth of P. digitatum was assessed according to the method of Tao et al. (Tao et al., 2014). The final concentrations of MNQ-CDs in the culture media were 0, 0.7, 1.4, 2.8, 5.6, 11.2, and 22.4 μg/mL, respectively. Then a conidia suspension was prepared. The conidia suspension was adjusted to 1 × 107 conidia/mL by using a hemocytometer with sterile distilled water. 10 μL conidia suspension (1 × 107 conidia/mL) was inoculated onto each petri dish. Finally, all petri dishes were cultured at 28 °C for 2 days. The lowest concentration that completely prevented fungal growth after 48 h of incubation at 28 °C was considered to be the MIC.

First, 2 mL conidia suspension (1 × 107 conidia/mL) was inoculated into potato dextrose broth and incubated for 48 h (28 °C, 140 rpm). Then, MNQ-CDs were dissolved in DMSO and added to conical bottles to achieve final concentrations of 2.8 µg/mL. The conical flask was incubated for another 48 h. Fungi without MNQ-CDs dose were considered untreated controls (Guo et al., 2019). Finally, the collected mycelium was rinsed three times with ice-cold Phosphate Buffered Saline (PBS, pH 7.4) and centrifuged at 3000×g for 5 min at 4 °C after each rinse, and the supernatant was completely discarded and collected in 1.5 mL centrifuge tubes.

The effect of MNQ-CDs on the surface morphology of P. digitatum was observed by SEM (Guo et al., 2020). The mycelia were fixed in 2.5% glutaraldehyde for 12 h at 4 °C, rinsed three times with 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4) for 10 min each time, and fixed for 1 h at room temperature in 1% osmium tetroxide (osmic acid) and rinsed three times with the same concentration of PBS for 10 min each. And then dehydrated in ethanol series for 10 min (concentrations were 30%, 45%, 60%, 75%, 85%, 95%, 100% and 100%, respectively), finally dried by critical point dryer with liquid carbon dioxide, gold-coated with stubs, observed and photographed by SEM (EVO MA-15, ZEISS, GER).

Total RNA was extracted from the mycelia using Trizol reagent (Invitrogen) as per the manufacturer's protocol. RNA concentration was determined using a Nanodrop ND-2000 system (Thermo Scientific, USA) based on the A260/A280 absorbance ratio, and the RNA quality was assessed using an Agilent Bioanalyzer 4150 system (Agilent Technologies, CA, USA). Qualified samples were required for the construction of the library. Paired-end libraries were prepared with 1 µg of RNA using ABclonal mRNA-seq Lib Prep Kit (ABclonal, China), PCR products were purified using AMPure XP system, and library quality was assessed by Agilent Bioanalyzer 4150 system. Finally, sequencing was performed using Illumina Novaseq 6000 instrument.

Raw data in fastq format were processed by internal perl script to obtain clean reads that could be used for subsequent analysis. Mapped reads were then obtained (with HISAT2 software) by clean reads compared to the orientation pattern of the reference genome, respectively (Kim et al., 2015). Compare with reference genome GTF/GFF files (using Gffcompare software). Uniquely mapped reads were counted using featureCounts, which are used to quantify the gene-level expression. Gene expression was calculated as FPKM (Li et al., 2021) with R package edgeR (version 3.8.5) based on the length of the gene and the reads mapped to that gene. Differential expression analysis between groups was identified using DESeq2 (Wang et al., 2021). P-value < 0.05 and |log2fold change (FC)| greater than 1 were the criteria for significant gene expression in this study.

To remove proteins and extract metabolites from the frozen mycelium, 800 μL of cold methanol/acetonitrile (1:1, v/v) was added to samples and adequately vortexed. Then, the samples were placed into a new centrifuge tube and centrifuge at 14,000 rcf for5 min at 4 °C. take the supernatant and freeze-dried, and store the samples at −80 °C. Add 100 μL of acetonitrile aqueous solution (acetonitrile: water = 1: 1, v/v) to reconstitute and vortex to extract metabolites, then centrifuge at 14,000 rcf for 15 min at 4 °C. The supernatant was collected for analysis.

The metabolites were separated by an ACQUIY UPLC BEH Amide column [100 × 2.1 mm, 1.8 µm (Waters, Ireland)] with the gradient elution of solvent A (25 mM ammonium acetate and 25 mM ammonium hydroxide in water) to solvent B (acetonitrile). Gradient elution program: 0–0.5 min, 95% B; 0.5–7 min, B from 95% linear to 65%; 7–8 min, B from 65% linear to 40%; 8–9 min, 40%B; 9–9.1 min, B linear from 40% to 95%; 9.1–12 min, 95% B. Samples are analyzed consecutively in random order to avoid the effects of fluctuations in the instrument detection signal. QC samples are inserted into the sample queue to monitor and evaluate system stability and data reliability. After the samples were separated, the metabolites were analyzed and identified by a Triple ToF 5600 mass spectrometer, and the samples were detected by electrospray ionization in positive and negative ion modes, with a scanning range of 50–1200 m/z.

For untargeted metabolomic analysis, raw data were converted to mzXML format using ProteoWizard, and the XCMS program was used for peak alignment, retention time correction and extraction of peak areas. Only the variables of non-zero measurements with more than 50% in at least one group were retained (Benton et al., 2015). Metabolites were identified by accurate mass matching (<25 ppm) and secondary spectrum matching according to the laboratory's self-built database. Further, the processed data were uploaded to SIMCA-P (version 14.1, Umetrics, Umea, Sweden), where they were subjected to multivariate analysis, including principal component analysis (PCA) and orthogonal partial least squares discriminant analysis (OPLS-DA), was performed. The 7-fold cross-validation and response permutation tests were used to assess model robustness. The variable importance in the projected (VIP) value of each variable in the OPLS-DA model was calculated to indicate its contribution to classification. The threshold was set at OPLS-DA VIP greater than 1 and P-value < 0.05 to generate a list of significant discriminant metabolites between different treatments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of MNQ-CDs on green mold of citrus fruits caused by P. digitatum
ce:section.ce:para: The citrus fruits used in this study were obtained from the Citrus tachibana orchard of Huizhou Niannianhong (Agriculture Co., ltd.), Huizhou City, China, and transported back to the laboratory immediately after harvesting. C. tachibana were selected with no damage lesions and a size of 6 ± 0.4 cm in diameter and soaked in 2% sodium hypochlorite solution for 15 min, then rinsed with water and dried naturally at room temperature. After drying, C. tachibana were stored for one night in an incubator at a temperature of 20 °C and relative humidity of 85% to 95%. Positive control drugs: Bellkute (biguanide trioctyl alkyl benzene sulfonate, 40% active ingredient), Imazalil (75% active ingredient).

The treatment was based on the method of Cai et al (2020) with a slight adjustment, C. tachibana were treated respectively with MNQ (100 μg/mL), MNQ-CDs (100 μg/mL), and Bellkute (25 μg/mL) and Imazalil (50 μg/mL) solutions for 5 min, dried, poked a wound (about 1 mm in diameter and 2 mm deep) in the center of the citrus flank, aspirated 5 μL of spore suspension (1 × 107 conidia/mL) and added dropwise to the dried wound, allowed to air dry, packed in sterilized disposable lunch boxes, and stored in an incubator at a temperature of 28℃ and relative humidity of 85% to 95% for 6 days. The results were observed and recorded every 24 h. Three replicates of six citruses per treatment were used. Citrus soaked in sterile water was used as a blank control.

The degree of citrus decay was expressed in terms of decay diameter and decay rate. The diameter was measured by the cross-cross method along the surface area of decay at the point of inception, and the decay diameter was calculated using the Eq. (1):
(1)
Decaydiameter
( m m )
=
∑ ( D e c a y d i a m e t e r )
Totalnumberofcitrus

Referring to the method of Chen et al (2006), the decay rate was calculated based on the size of the decay fraction on the peel: 0 = no decay; 1 = 0–25%; 2 = 25–50%; 3 = 50%-75%; 4 ≥ 75%; 5 = complete decay. The decay rate was calculated using the Eq. (2):
(2)
D e c a y r a t e
%
= ∑
Decayfraction × T h e n u m b e r o f c i t r u s c o r r e s p o n d i n g t o t h i s f r a c t i o n
Highestdecayfraction × T o t a l n u m b e r o f c i t r u s
× 100 %

Graph Pad Prism 8 software was used to plot the data; one-way ANOVA was performed using SPSS 25.0 software and Duncan's multiple comparisons were used to analyze the significance of differences, with p < 0.05 indicating significant differences.

The MIC of MNQ-CDs was 2.8 µg/mL (Fig. 1 A). To study the effect of MNQ-CDs on P. digitatum, the mycelia treated with 2.8 μg/mL MNQ-CDs (hereinafter referred to as D-T) were compared with those without MNQ-CDs treatment (hereinafter referred to as D-C). The morphology of mycelium was observed by SEM, and MNQ-CDs had an obvious influence on the morphology of P. digitatum. The mycelia in D-C have a round surface, regular shape and complete structure (Fig. 1B). However, the mycelium surface in D-T collapsed severely and showed multiple folds. The structure was irregular and distorted, with convex and dry, and the sporangium atrophied.

To explore the changes in the mycelial transcriptome treated with MNQ-CDs, transcriptome analysis was performed. A sum of 10, 103 genes (441 differential genes) were found in groups D-C and D-T. In D-T, 317 genes were up-regulated and 124 genes down-regulated (Table S1). A study of enrichment GO terms was conducted to assess the effect of MNQ-CDs on the mycelial (Fig. 2 A). A majority of differential genes that were upregulated belong to biological processes (BP) and molecular functions (MF), but the numbers were slightly different after treated with MNQ-CDs. In the BP categories, the most prominent terms are “oxidation-reduction process ”, “drug catabolic processes” and “transmembrane transport”. There was an overall enrichment of catalytic genes (oxidoreductase activity, catalase activity, catalytic activity, etc.) among the MF categories (FDR < 0.05). It was demonstrated that MNQ-CDs could significantly alter the permeability of cell membrane and catalytic process of substances in P. digitatum, and alter the catabolic process of drugs in P. digitatum.

Subsequently, KEGG analysis was performed on 441 differential genes in D-T to capture the transcriptional alterations of the metabolic pathway (Table S2). The enriched pathways included 11 in total, including 3 amino acids metabolic pathways and 2 drug metabolic pathways (p < 0.05), of which amino acid metabolic pathways were up-regulated, while drug metabolic pathways were down-regulated, suggesting that MNQ-CDs could enhance amino acid metabolism of P. digitatum and attenuate drug metabolism. In the scatter diagram of the KEGG pathway of total differential genes (Fig. 2B), the Metabolic pathway of P. digitatum had the most significant enrichment effect, and 47 differential genes were involved in this pathway.

After MNQ-CDs treatment, mycelia were collected and subjected to Ultrahigh-Performance Liquid chromatography Quadrupole-time-of-flight mass spectrometry. PCA and OPLS-DA were performed on all data to evaluate the technology differences (Fig. S1). There are 122 differential metabolites in total in D-T (Table S3), with 25 increased and 97 decreased. A total of 8 major metabolic groups were distinguished, which were amino acids, lipids, nucleotides, peptides, xenobiotics, carbohydrates, cofactors/vitamins, and energy (Fig. 3 A). Lipids (41.80%) were the most variable group, and MNQ-CDs treatment-induced and inhibited the most in PC (16:0e/5,6-EET) (3.00-fold increase) and 9(Z),11(E),13(E)-Octadecatrienoic acid ethyl ester (reduced by 65.64%); followed by peptides (22.13%) and amino acids (16.39%), with 96.30% peptides and 100.00% amino acids content decreased. The MNQ-CDs treatment inhibited different amino acids including glutamate, valine, leucine, lysine, phenylalanine and ornithine, among which, valine, leucine and phenylalanine were inhibited by more than 50% by the MNQ-CDs treatment. In conclusion, the synthesis of most lipids and amino acids was inhibited at D-T indicating that MNQ-CDs stress P. digitatum mainly through the inhibition of lipid and amino acids synthesis.

KEGG pathway enrichment analysis was performed for 122 differential metabolites in D-T, involving 49 pathways, among which 4 pathways (P < 0.05) were significantly enriched (Fig. 3B). These are lipid metabolic pathways (alpha-Linolenic acid metabolism), signal transduction pathways (Neuroactive ligand-receptor interaction), cofactors and vitamins (Riboflavin metabolism), and amino acids (Arginine biosynthesis).

According to the differential genes and differential metabolites, the correlation analysis of the differential genes and differential metabolites in D-T was carried out by the “spearman” algorithm (Fig. S2), further highlighting the association of each transcript with a specific metabolite. Network analysis of differential metabolites and genes was performed to further study the antifungal mechanism of MNQ-CDs (Pearson correlation coefficient = 1 or = -1, P-value < 0.05). It was found that trans-traumatic acid and methyl jasmonate were related to alpha-Linolenic acid metabolism (Fig. 4 A), and Lumichrome and Riboflavin were related to riboflavin metabolism (Fig. 4B). At the same time, Phenylacetaldehyde (related to phenylalanine metabolism), Serotonin (related to tryptophan metabolism) and Tyramine (related to tyrosine metabolism) all belong to aromatic amino acids (AAAs) metabolism (Fig. 4C). In addition, Emetine is related to shikimate metabolism, which is an important way to generate aromatic amino acids. The results showed that MNQ-CDs could regulate the co-expression of differential genes and differential metabolites related to alpha-Linolenic acid metabolism, riboflavin metabolism, and aromatic amino acid metabolism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of MNQ-CDs on the control of green mold of citrus fruits caused by P. digitatum
ce:section.ce:para: Under the treatment concentration of 100 μg/mL, there was no phytotoxicity occurred in any of the treatment groups, and the occurrence of citrus fruit green mold was controlled to varying degrees within 6 days after inoculation (Fig. 5 A). Both MNQ and MNQ-CDs were effective in controlling the infestation of P. digitatum on citrus fruits and inhibiting the expansion of citrus fruit decay diameter, and both treatment groups were effective in controlling the decay rate of citrus fruits at the early stage of the disease. The fruit decay rates of MNQ and MNQ-CDs treatments were significantly lower than those of the control group (p < 0.05) within days 2 to 6 of treatment (Fig. 5B), and MNQ-CDs treatment was significantly more effective than MNQ treatment. The diameter of fruit spots was significantly smaller than that of the control (p < 0.05) from days 3 to 6 of treatment (Fig. 5C). During the 6-day observation period, the decay rate and decay diameter of citrus in both control and treated groups increased with time, but decay rate and decay diameter of citrus in MNQ-CDs group were always lower than the control group.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Unexpected changes in the antifungal effect of MNQ-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The antibacterial and antifungal effects of CDs depend on the one hand on its small size, high surface energy and easy adsorption on the cell surface. CDs disrupts the cell membrane by electrostatic interaction with the cell membrane, causing the cell contents to be disrupted and leak. On the other hand, when CDs enter cells, photocatalytic generation of ROS occurs under light conditions, which disrupts cell physiology, including lipids, proteins, nucleotides and metabolites. In recent years, researchers have synthesized CDs with different shapes, heteroatom doping, and surface functionalization by adjusting CDs and preparation methods to modify surface groups, charges, and reactive oxygen yields and promote antibacterial and antifungal activity. Red fluorescent emission CDs (R-CDs) were synthesized using fungicides as carbon sources by Liu et al (2021). The synthesized R-CDs not only have inherent antibacterial activity, but also can effectively kill a variety of drug-resistant bacteria by photoexcitation at 590 nm to produce ROS, and the synthesis of CDs effectively improves the antibacterial activity of fungicides. In addition, other studies have shown that CDs synthesized with antibiotics (such as ciprofloxacin hydrochloride and metronidazole) not only show good bactericidal activity, but also reduce their drug resistance (Liang et al., 2020).

The MIC of MNQ-CDs was 2.8 µg/mL (Fig. 1A). Previously, our team found that the MIC of MNQ on P. digitatum was 5 µg/mL (Guo et al., 2020), indicating that after solvothermal treatment, the antifungal properties of MNQ-CDs are enhanced. This is mutually corroborated with the results of the control effect of MNQ-CDs on citrus fruit green mold. Compared with MNQ, MNQ-CDs had a better control effect on the green mold of citrus fruits, and could more significantly inhibit the propagation of P. digitatum (Fig. 5). These indicate that CDs derived from known antifungal compounds may be an unexpected new source of novel, highly effective antifungal materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antifungal mechanism of MNQ-CDs against P. digitatum
ce:section.ce:para: Amino acids are the monomers that make up proteins and have various functions such as signaling, nitrogen homeostasis, and participation in the biosynthesis of nucleotides, antioxidants (glutathione), glucosamine and polyamines (Liu et al., 2017). Numerous studies have shown that amino acid-related metabolic processes are critical for the propagation and virulence of pathogenic fungi. AAAs include phenylalanine, tryptophan, and tyrosine, and the metabolic pathways of phenylalanine, tryptophan, and tyrosine were significantly up-regulated in the transcriptome results, indicating a large number of aromatic amino acids broken down by metabolism. In the metabolomic results, many amino acids such as glutamate, valine, leucine, lysine, phenylalanine and ornithine levels were significantly decreased. The metabolism of lipids and carbohydrates requires the participation of phenylalanine, which is catalyzed by hydroxylase to produce tyrosine, which contributes to the synthesis of hormones and neurotransmitters. Phenylalanine is a common precursor of many phenolic compounds, including flavonoids, condensed tannins, lignin, and phenylpropane/benzene compounds, among others. Tyrosine has an important function in the metabolic process and is the precursor of isoquinoline alkaloids, pigment betalines and quinones. Tryptophan is the precursor of alkaloids, plant alkaloids, and indole glucosinolates. Tryptophan can be converted into 5-hydroxytryptamine, which is an important neurotransmitter. The reduction of AAAs affects multiple metabolic pathways of lipids, carbohydrates, etc.

Branched-chain amino acids (BCAAs) include valine, leucine, and isoleucine. AAAs are synthesized in vivo by the shikimate pathway, and in the process of shikimate production, erythrose 4-phosphate and phosphoenolpyruvate (PEP) is catalyzed to generate shikimate. PEP is also catalyzed to generate pyruvate, which can generate branched-chain amino acids (Fig. S3). We speculate that due to the up-regulation of AAAs metabolism genes, the shikimic acid pathway is promoted to generate AAAs, resulting in the reduction of pyruvate generated from PEP, and the reduced generation of BCAAs. Secondly, the metabolism of AAAs can generate fumaric acid, which promotes TAC, so BCAAs are forced to be converted into substances required in TAC, which further reduces the content of BCAAs. BCAAs are essential amino acids in biology, which not only promote nitrogen retention and protein synthesis, but also have special physiological and biological functions and have an important function in promoting metabolic homeostasis. BCAAs can also be used as an energy source for organisms, and under adverse conditions, BCAAs can enhance oxidation to provide energy to maintain the normal life activities of the organism (Fontana et al., 2016). Valine is a glucogenic amino acid that is oxidatively dehydrogenated to enter the glucose pathway for metabolism and can also be degraded to succinyl coenzyme A. Leucine is the only ketogenic amino acid that can enter the lipid pathway for metabolism and can also be degraded to acetoacetate and acetyl coenzyme a. Coenzyme A is a pantothenic acid-containing coenzyme that acts as a carrier for acyl groups in certain enzymatic reactions and is an important factor in the regulation of carbohydrate, lipid and protein metabolism. Glutamate has an important function in protein metabolism in living organisms and is involved in many important chemical reactions in animals, plants and microorganisms. Lysine is involved in the biosynthesis of carnitine in the body, and carnitine has an important function in lipid metabolism and is an essential cofactor for lipid metabolism. Ornithine and arginine, glutamic acid, and proline can be converted into each other, and combined with benzoic acid, phenylacetic acid, etc. for detoxification. The reduced content of these amino acids demonstrates that MNQ-CDs have a significant impact on metabolism.

Lipids and ergosterol are the basic components of cell membranes. Xin et al showed that the antifungal mechanism of Momordica charantia L. extract was due to its ability to disrupt the integrity of cell membranes, which were mainly affected by total lipid and ergosterol content in the cell membrane (Xin et al., 2019). Helal et al proposed that reducing lipids hinders the transport of lipid-soluble substances and disrupts cell-selective permeability (Helal et al., 2007). In D-T, we found a large amount of lipid content down-regulation, indicating that MNQ-CDs reduced the total lipid content of P. digitatum, affected the stability of the cell membrane and increased its fluidity, indicating that the membrane integrity was impaired. The decrease in lipid content reflects irreversible damage to the cell membrane, so we strongly speculate that the cell membrane may be the target of MNQ-CDs.

Among the metabolites, alpha-Linolenic acid was decreased by 25%, trans-traumatic acid was decreased by 40%, and methyl Jasmonate was decreased by 50%. Alpha-Linolenic acid maintains the relative fluidity of cell membranes to ensure the normal physiological functions of cells. trans-traumatic acid is produced by eighteen-carbon unsaturated fatty acids (such as Linoleic acid and Linolenic acid) in the cell membrane after the cell membrane is damaged. It is an important signal molecule of the callus response and can induce the transcription of related genes. Methyl Jasmonate can induce the production of ergosterol in fungi, and ergosterol has an important function in maintaining cell viability, membrane integrity and fluidity (Helal et al., 2007). Both trans-traumatic acid and methyl jasmonate are produced by the alpha-Linolenic acid metabolism, thus the reduction of alpha-Linolenic acid also weakens the cell viability and damage repair function of the cell membrane. It is inferred that MNQ-CD treatment has a certain effect on membrane lipid composition, cell membrane permeability and cell membrane damage repair. The morphological integrity of fungal hyphae is essential for their survival (Gao et al., 2018). After the destruction of the cell membrane, the synthesis and transport pathways of intracellular substances may also be affected, thus hindering the normal physiological metabolism of cells. The amino acid content of the metabolites of P. digitatum decreased significantly in D-T, which may be due to the increased permeability of the cell membrane, resulting in an increased outflow of amino acids. To verify this conclusion, the cell structure of P. digitatum was observed by Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Fig. 6 ). It can be seen that the thickness of the cell wall of the D-C hyphae was uniform, the cell wall and cell membrane were smooth and regular, and the cell wall and vacuoles were visible. The cell wall of D-T mycelia became thinner, the outer edge was not clear, the cell membrane was irregularly bent, the cytoplasmic wall separation phenomenon was vaguely appearing, a large number of cell contents flowed out, the amorphous electron compacts increased, the cytoplasm appeared a lot of blanks, and the cell damage was serious. The results of the electron microscope were consistent with the expectation.

At the same time, MNQ-CDs significantly down-regulated the metabolism of cytochrome P450 (CYP450) to exogenous drugs. CYP450 is an important factor in the metabolism of drugs and endogenous substances, drug metabolism pathways, the synthesis of metabolites and affects the toxicity of drugs. It is a key enzyme in the process of drug metabolism, which can convert drugs from hydrophobic to hydrophilic, making them easier to metabolize, and playing an important role in P. digitatum to cope with the stress of MNQ-CDs. Retinol can be metabolized to form retinoic acid, which binds to nuclear receptors (retinoic acid receptors, retinoid X receptors) and induces the expression and activity of CYP450 enzymes. The reduction of 13-cis-retinol (35.47% reduction) inhibited the expression and activity of CYP450. The active region of CYP450 is composed of a phenylalanine cluster (composed of 7 phenylalanine residues) and the active site of CYP450, and some of the phenylalanine residues constituting the phenylalanine cluster can affect the CYP450 activity was further inhibited by decreased phenylalanine content in metabolites. In addition, most CYP450 contains 2 charges, which need to be delivered by riboflavin containing [2Fe-2S] clusters, to undergo redox reactions in each metabolic, cyclic reaction. The accumulation of riboflavin (1.8-fold increase) indicated that most of the CYP450 were not bound to riboflavin and were transported to the site for drug metabolism to react. The reduction of the metabolism of cytochrome P450 to exogenous drugs makes P. digitatum unable to cope with the stress of MNQ-CDs, and gradually tends to death.

After combining the metabolites with their related genes and pathways, it was concluded that MNQ-CDs inhibited the metabolism of cytochrome P450 to exogenous drugs, inhibited pathways such as amino acid metabolism and lipid metabolism, and damaged cell membranes, and caused irreversible cell damage. Eventually cell necrosis is due to a lack of protective tissue.

Plant essential oils mainly have the following mechanisms of action on fungi. The first is to destroy the morphological structure of fungal cells or mycelium, such as cell membrane, cell wall, cytoplasm and other basic structures, induce mycelium lysis, and cause irreversible cell damage (Shao et al., 2013). The second is to disrupt the cellular metabolic system, such as inhibiting the biosynthesis of mycelial DNA, RNA, lipids and proteins, and the related genes cannot be expressed, which in turn leads to the loss of regulatory control of metabolism and the normal conduct of self-replication, ultimately leading to cell necrosis(Li et al., 2020). The third is to inhibit the production and germination of thalli and conidia, to block the generation of the progeny of pathogenic thalli and make the thalli gradually senile and die. There are differences in the mechanism of action of different substances. Some studies have shown that the antifungal mechanism of essential oils is not a single pathway, but the synergistic result of multiple pathways (Sperotto et al., 2013).

In the preliminary research of our team, we found that the mechanism of MNQ is similar to that of plant essential oils. MNQ-CDs inhibit amino acid metabolism, lipid metabolism and other pathways formed by the cell membrane, and induce mycelial dissolution, and cell membrane damage, resulting in irreversible cell damage, which is similar to the mechanism of citral. Through the cell wall of fungi, it specifically prevented the synthesis of glucan and chitin in the cell wall. The cell wall was damaged and weakened the P. digitatum protection function of the cell membrane, the cell membrane was damaged. Finally, cell necrosis is due to a lack of protective tissue. The mechanism of action is similar to that of tea tree oil (Shao et al., 2013). It is speculated that the MNQ-CDs and MNQ have other antifungal activity mechanisms. GC/MS, fluorescence microplate reader, flow cytometer and other equipment can be used to determine the structural sites of the special chemical bonds in MNQ-CDs acting on pathogenic mycelium cells and spores, and identify the functional substances changed in the cells after the action, and explore the molecular biological mechanism of MNQ-CDs inhibiting pathogens.

Some studies have shown that combinations of key compounds and other essential oils may have synergistic or enhancing effects, thus combining essential oils provides better antifungal effects than using them alone (Hossain et al., 2016). Therefore, we can combine MNQ-CDs with other antifungal compounds to synergistically produce a variety of antifungal mechanisms and inhibit the propagation and reproduction of strains in all directions. For example, citronellal can inhibit the spore germination of P. digitatum, and the combination of MNQ-CDs+ citronellal can synergistically inhibit spore germination of P. digitatum, interfering with the formation of the cell wall, and destroy the integrity of cell membrane, comprehensively inhibit the propagation and reproduction of strains.

The antifungal activity of MNQ-CDs against P. digitatum has been demonstrated. The MIC of MNQ-CDs was 2.8 µg/m. The antifungal effect of MNQ-CDs is better than that of MNQ. After MNQ-CDs treatment, drug metabolism, amino acid metabolism, lipid metabolism and signal transduction function of P. digitatum were significantly changed, lipid and amino acid contents were significantly reduced, and synthesis of the cell membrane was blocked. In addition, using TEM, it can be observed that the cell wall and membrane were damaged, and a large number of cell contents flowed out, resulting in a large amount of blank cytoplasm, serious cell damage and significantly increased cell necrosis. We infer that it may be due to after treated with MNQ-CDs, the drug metabolism, amino acid metabolism, carbohydrate metabolism and function of signal transduction of P. digitatum, were significantly influenced, the drug metabolism was decreased, the integrity of the mycelium of P. digitatum (such as the cell membrane and cell wall structure) was destroyed, cell permeability increases, small molecules and ions leakage, and eventually cell necrosis. Compared with MNQ, MNQ-CDs had a better control effect on the green mold of citrus fruits, and could more significantly inhibit the propagation of P. digitatum, indicating that CDs derived from known antifungal compounds may be an unexpected new source of novel, highly effective antifungal materials. In this study, transcriptomics and metabolomics were used to analyze the mechanism of inhibition of P. digitatum by MNQ-CDs, to further using GC/MS, fluorescence microplate reader, flow cytometer and other equipment can be used to determine the structural sites of the special chemical bonds in MNQ-CDs acting on pathogenic mycelium cells and spores, and identify the functional substances changed in the cells after the action, and explore the molecular biological mechanism of MNQ-CDs inhibiting pathogens. The research results can enrich the biological theory that natural compounds damage cell structure and metabolism, and provide technical support for reducing post-harvest loss and increasing fruit added value. This research has important theoretical and practical significance.

Yongchun Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Wei Li: Methodology, Writing – original draft. Keyue Wu: Methodology. Bingfu Lei: Methodology. Jianying Chen: Methodology. Xiaoyong Zhang: Methodology, Funding acquisition. Hongtao Lei: Methodology, Funding acquisition. Xuewu Duan: Methodology. Riming Huang: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Supervision, Project administration, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China, grant number 32161160304; the Key-Area Research and Development Program of Guangdong Province, grant number 2020B1111030004; the Program of Department of Natural Resources of Guangdong Province, grant number GDNRC[2021]53, and the Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Province, grant number 2021GXNSFDA075010.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2023.135687.

ce:section.ce:para:
